wins.title: Justice Is Done
wins.plotSummary: A woman stands trial for the mercy killing of her terminally ill employer at his own request, and the film follows the seven jurors chosen to decide her fate, each bringing their own private prejudices and life experiences into the jury room. As deliberations unfold, personal grudges, faith, class resentment, and unrelated domestic troubles color each juror's reading of the same facts. André Cayatte's courtroom drama uses the case to expose how thoroughly "justice" is shaped by the flawed, biased humans tasked with dispensing it.

wins.title: Rashomon
wins.plotSummary: A woodcutter, a priest, and a bystander take shelter from the rain at the ruined Rashomon gate, recounting the baffling trial testimony surrounding a samurai's murder and his wife's assault in a forest. Four witnesses, including the accused bandit, the wife, the dead samurai speaking through a medium, and the woodcutter himself, each tell a contradictory version of events that flatters their own conduct. Akira Kurosawa's landmark film uses the clashing accounts to question whether objective truth is even possible when every witness reshapes reality to preserve their own self-image.

wins.title: Forbidden Games
wins.plotSummary: During the chaotic French exodus of 1940, five-year-old Paulette is orphaned when her parents and dog are killed in a German air attack, and she's taken in by the peasant family of ten-year-old Michel. The two children cope with the surrounding death by secretly building a pet cemetery in an abandoned watermill, escalating to stealing crosses from a real graveyard to mark their makeshift graves. René Clément's war drama uses the children's solemn, innocent ritual to reflect the adult world's violence back at itself, ending in Paulette's wrenching separation from the one friend who understood her grief.

wins.title: Romeo and Juliet
wins.plotSummary: Renato Castellani's adaptation of Shakespeare's tragedy follows the star-crossed young lovers of feuding Verona families, cast largely with inexperienced actors and shot on authentic Italian locations for a naturalistic, unglamorized take on the doomed romance. Laurence Harvey's Romeo and newcomer Susan Shentall's Juliet fall instantly in love despite their families' violent rivalry, setting in motion the secret marriage and tragic misunderstandings that doom them both. Castellani's version leans into period detail and location authenticity over theatrical polish, earning acclaim abroad despite disappointing box office.

wins.title: Ordet
wins.plotSummary: In a devout rural Danish family, patriarch Morten's three sons each embody a different relationship to faith: the eldest has lost his; the middle son believes himself to be the second coming of Christ after a religious crisis; and the youngest wants to marry into a rival, stricter sect that Morten disdains. As a family tragedy pushes everyone's beliefs to their breaking point, the seemingly deluded middle son's unwavering faith becomes the film's central, startling test of whether miracles are still possible. Carl Theodor Dreyer's austere drama builds to one of cinema's most famous and controversial acts of faith.

wins.title: Aparajito
wins.plotSummary: The second chapter of Satyajit Ray's Apu Trilogy follows young Apu and his mother as they relocate to the holy city of Varanasi after his father's death, and later to a village where Apu's academic gifts pull him toward a wider world his mother can't follow. As Apu grows into adolescence and wins a scholarship to study in Calcutta, his ambitions increasingly separate him from his aging, lonely mother, who remains behind in the village. Ray's unsentimental portrait of a widening gulf between mother and son was criticized in India for its unconventional emotional restraint but won acclaim internationally for its honesty.

wins.title: Rickshaw Man
wins.plotSummary: Matsugoro, an illiterate but big-hearted rickshaw puller nicknamed "Wild Matsu" for his hot temper, becomes an unlikely father figure to a young boy after the child's father dies, forming a bond with the boy and his widowed mother that shapes the rest of his life. Though never able to cross the class divide to express his growing feelings for the widow, Matsu devotes himself to the boy's upbringing from the sidelines, watching him grow into a young man while his own rough, working-class life continues in the shadows. Hiroshi Inagaki's remake of his own earlier film follows Matsu's quiet, self-sacrificing devotion across decades.

wins.title: General Della Rovere
wins.plotSummary: In Nazi-occupied Genoa, small-time con man Emanuele Bardone is coerced by German forces into impersonating a captured Resistance leader, General Della Rovere, in order to infiltrate a prison and identify other partisans. As Bardone plays the role among genuine resistance fighters who look to him for leadership and courage, the impersonation gradually becomes real, and he finds himself transforming into the hero he was only pretending to be. Roberto Rossellini's drama follows Bardone's unexpected moral awakening to its ultimate test: whether he'll betray the real resistance leader to save himself, or die as the man he's become.

wins.title: The Great War
wins.plotSummary: Two mismatched, cowardly Italian soldiers, the scheming Roman Oreste and the gruff Milanese Giovanni, meet during World War I conscription and forge an uneasy friendship built entirely on their shared desire to avoid danger and survive the war unscathed. Bouncing between training, combat, and rare leave, the two witness the catastrophic Italian defeat at Caporetto and are assigned as expendable relay runners, the army's most dangerous job, precisely because they're considered its least capable soldiers. Mario Monicelli's tragicomedy plays their cowardice for laughs until the war's grim reality forces an unexpectedly sobering final test of courage.

wins.title: Tomorrow Is My Turn
wins.plotSummary: After France falls to Germany in 1940, two French soldiers, Roger and his companions, are conscripted as forced laborers on a German farm under the STO program, becoming unexpectedly entangled in the lives, and affections, of the German family holding them. As the war drags on, loyalties blur between captor and captive, complicating any clean return to the men's old lives once liberation finally comes. André Cayatte's drama follows the messy, human aftermath of occupation from the rare vantage of the conscripted laborers themselves rather than the resistance or the front lines.

wins.title: Last Year at Marienbad
wins.plotSummary: In an ornate baroque hotel, a man approaches a woman insisting they met and fell in love the year before at a similar resort, and that she asked him to wait a year before running away together; she insists she has never met him. A second man, possibly her husband, repeatedly asserts quiet dominance over the pair by beating the first man at a game of Nim, as the three drift through the hotel's identical corridors and manicured gardens. Alain Resnais's puzzle-box film deliberately never resolves whether the affair happened, was imagined, or is happening now, using disorienting repetition and contradiction as its entire subject.

wins.title: Family Diary
wins.plotSummary: In 1945 Rome, struggling journalist Enrico learns of the sudden death of his estranged younger brother Lorenzo and looks back on their difficult shared history: raised apart, Enrico by their poor grandmother and Lorenzo by a wealthy aristocrat, before reuniting as adults in 1930s Florence. Enrico becomes his more privileged, self-destructive brother's reluctant keeper, their bond twisted by a lifetime of guilt, resentment, and love neither can fully express. Valerio Zurlini's drama, adapted from Vasco Pratolini's autobiographical novel, mourns a relationship whose reconciliation only becomes clear once it's too late.

wins.title: Ivan's Childhood
wins.plotSummary: Ivan, a 12-year-old boy orphaned when German forces killed his family, works as a scout for the Soviet Army, slipping across enemy lines on dangerous reconnaissance missions that no child should be asked to undertake. Between missions, sympathetic officers try in vain to send him away from the front to safety, but Ivan, hollowed out by grief and hatred, refuses to abandon his one-man war of vengeance. Andrei Tarkovsky's debut feature intercuts Ivan's brutal wartime reality with tender, dreamlike flashbacks to the peaceful childhood the war has destroyed.

wins.title: Hands over the City
wins.plotSummary: Ruthless Naples real-estate developer and city councilman Edoardo Nottola exploits his political power to push through a lucrative suburban development deal, disregarding the safety and displacement of the city's poor. When a nearby residential building collapses at a construction site tied to his son's company, idealistic communist councilman De Vita launches an inquiry into Nottola's corruption, threatening to expose the collusion between capital and city government. Francesco Rosi's docudrama follows the investigation as a portrait of how political power protects itself, even as evidence of wrongdoing mounts.

wins.title: Red Desert
wins.plotSummary: In the industrial wasteland surrounding Ravenna, Giuliana, still fragile after a car accident, drifts through her days unable to find footing in a world of petrochemical plants, toxic landscapes, and her own numbing anxiety. Her husband Ugo's visiting business associate Corrado, restless in his own way, becomes drawn to Giuliana's fragility, offering a connection that never quite resolves her dislocation from her surroundings. Michelangelo Antonioni's first color film uses industrial Italy's toxic, alien beauty as the external expression of Giuliana's fractured inner state.

wins.title: Sandra
wins.plotSummary: Sandra returns to her family's ancestral home in Volterra for a ceremony honoring her Jewish father, who died in a Nazi concentration camp after being denounced, she suspects, by her own mother and stepfather. Her return reignites a charged, incestuous intimacy with her brother Gianni that unsettles her unsuspecting husband, as the siblings quietly plot revenge against the family members they hold responsible for their father's death. Luchino Visconti's modern retelling of the Electra myth turns a homecoming into a slow-burning reckoning with inherited betrayal.

wins.title: The Battle of Algiers
wins.plotSummary: Between 1954 and 1957, FLN guerrilla fighter Ali La Pointe rises through the Algerian resistance in Algiers, organizing a campaign of bombings and assassinations against French colonial rule that draws a brutal crackdown from French paratroopers. Shot in stark documentary-style black and white with mostly non-professional actors who lived through the real events, the film traces the escalating cycle of violence and torture from both the FLN and French forces without flinching from either side. Gillo Pontecorvo's film ends with Algeria's eventual independence, framing the brutal battle as a costly, necessary chapter in a longer war the French ultimately lost.

wins.title: Belle de Jour
wins.plotSummary: Séverine, a beautiful but sexually repressed young housewife unable to be intimate with her devoted husband, secretly begins working afternoons at a high-class Parisian brothel under the alias "Belle de Jour," acting out elaborate fantasies of submission she can't voice at home. As her double life deepens, one of her clients grows dangerously obsessed with her, threatening to expose the secret and destroy the marriage her daytime fantasies were never meant to touch. Luis Buñuel's surrealist drama blurs fantasy and reality throughout, leaving Séverine's true experience deliberately ambiguous by the film's unsettling end.

wins.title: Artists Under the Big Top: Perplexed
wins.originalTitle: Die Artisten in der Zirkuskuppel: ratlos
wins.plotSummary: Leni Peickert, heir to a failing traditional circus, dreams of reinventing it into something more artistically ambitious and socially relevant, but struggles to find backers, performers, or an audience willing to embrace her vision. Alexander Kluge intercuts Leni's quixotic effort with newsreel footage, philosophical quotations, and documentary-style digressions, using the circus's collapse as a stand-in for the difficulty of building something genuinely new within a hidebound institution. The film's fragmented, essayistic style mirrors Kluge's own frustrated ambitions for reinventing German cinema itself.

wins.title: Atlantic City
wins.plotSummary: Sally, a young casino worker in Atlantic City, becomes entangled with Lou, an aging small-time numbers runner, after her estranged husband is killed by mobsters over a stolen bag of cocaine. Lou seizes the drugs and, hoping to impress Sally, starts selling them himself, drawing the attention of the same criminals. When gangsters ransack Sally's apartment searching for the cocaine, Lou kills them to protect her, and the two flee together with the proceeds. Sally eventually leaves for a new life in France while Lou returns to his old world, once again looking after Grace, the faded former beauty queen he has long cared for.

wins.title: Gloria
wins.plotSummary: In the South Bronx, former mob girlfriend Gloria Swenson is unwillingly drawn into protecting a neighbor's young son, Phil, after his family is marked for death by mobsters seeking a ledger that could incriminate them. Gloria and Phil go on the run through New York City, dodging gunmen while an unlikely bond forms between the tough, streetwise woman and the frightened boy. After a series of narrow escapes and shootouts, Gloria confronts the mob boss directly to trade the ledger for their safety. The film closes with Gloria and Phil reuniting after he flees to Pittsburgh.

wins.title: Marianne and Juliane
wins.plotSummary: Two sisters take opposite paths toward the same feminist and political convictions: Juliane becomes a journalist campaigning for women's rights, while Marianne joins a violent left-wing terrorist group. When Marianne abandons her young son and her husband dies, Juliane reluctantly takes over as the boy's guardian, even as she keeps visiting her estranged sister in prison. After Marianne dies in custody under disputed circumstances officially ruled a suicide, Juliane becomes convinced she was murdered and tries in vain to draw public attention to the case. Years later, following an attack connected to his mother's notoriety, Marianne's now-grown son is finally reconciled with his aunt.

wins.title: The State of Things
studios: Pro-ject Filmproduktion im Verlag der Autoren
wins.plotSummary: A film crew shooting a black-and-white science-fiction picture on the Portuguese coast is forced to halt production when they run out of money and film stock. Stranded together in an empty hotel, the cast and crew drift into idle conversation and introspection while waiting for word from their absent producer, Gordon. Frustrated by the silence, director Friedrich Munro travels to Los Angeles to track Gordon down himself, only to find him hiding out in a camper van, on the run from gangsters he owes money to.

wins.title: First Name: Carmen
wins.plotSummary: Carmen, a young woman involved in a gang of thieves, borrows her uncle's seaside apartment to plan a bank robbery, while a string quartet is heard rehearsing Beethoven throughout the film. During the heist she falls for Joseph, a bank guard, and the two begin a turbulent love affair even as Carmen warns him that loving her will destroy him. After Joseph is arrested and later acquitted, he reunites with Carmen, but her gang increasingly shuts him out of their plans. The film ends violently during a botched kidnapping, with Joseph confronting Carmen and a shot fired between them.

wins.title: A Year of the Quiet Sun
wins.plotSummary: In 1946 Poland, a war widow named Emilia scrapes by baking cookies to sell while caring for her dying mother. Her life changes when she meets Norman, an American soldier stationed there to help exhume the graves of Allied airmen, and the two form a tender, halting romance despite the language barrier between them. When Norman is ordered home, Emilia is faced with a chance to emigrate with him but gives up her only spot on a smuggler's passage to a friend instead. Decades later, an elderly Emilia, now living in a convent, receives news of an inheritance from Norman in America but collapses before she can reach him.

wins.title: Vagabond
wins.plotSummary: The film opens with the discovery of a young drifter, Mona Bergeron, found frozen to death in a ditch in rural France, then reconstructs her final winter through the recollections of people who crossed her path. Mona survives by hitchhiking, camping, and taking odd jobs, having abandoned a conventional life in Paris in search of total freedom. As winter deepens, she loses what little she has, including her tent, and grows increasingly desperate and isolated. The film closes as it began, with Mona succumbing to cold and exhaustion in a vineyard.

wins.title: The Green Ray
wins.plotSummary: Delphine, recently single after her partner and travel companion breaks off their plans, struggles to enjoy a summer vacation on her own. She drifts between a seaside gathering where she feels out of place, a family invitation to Ireland she declines, and a solitary trip to the Alps that leaves her unfulfilled, all while resisting the superficial rituals of dating. Along the way she hears about the "green ray," a rare optical phenomenon at sunset said to reveal one's true feelings. At a train station she meets a young man, and together they set off to watch for it, hinting at a possible new connection.

wins.title: Au revoir les enfants
wins.plotSummary: Set in occupied France during the winter of 1943-44, the film follows Julien Quentin, a boy at a Catholic boarding school, who at first resents a new classmate, Jean Bonnet. Julien gradually discovers that Bonnet is actually a Jewish boy named Jean Kippelstein, hidden at the school by its compassionate headmaster, and the two become close friends. When the Gestapo raids the school, an inadvertent glance from Julien reveals Bonnet's hiding place, leading to the arrest of the headmaster and the Jewish students. An epilogue reveals their deaths in the camps, with an older Julien reflecting that he will never forget that day.

wins.title: The Legend of the Holy Drinker
wins.plotSummary: Andreas, a homeless alcoholic living under the bridges of Paris, is given 200 francs by a mysterious stranger who asks only that he repay the sum to a local church once his fortunes improve. Determined to honor his word, Andreas repeatedly tries to save the money, but a string of chance encounters, temptations, and setbacks keep pulling him back into destitution. The film follows his wandering, bittersweet attempts at redemption as he searches for a way to fulfill his debt.

wins.title: A City of Sadness
wins.plotSummary: The film follows the Lin family in the Taiwanese port town of Keelung in the years after Japan's surrender in World War II, as they run a nightclub amid the chaos of postwar smuggling and gang violence. A nurse named Hinomi grows close to Wen-ching, the family's deaf-mute fourth son, against the backdrop of escalating tension between Taiwanese residents and the incoming Nationalist government. When the February 28 Incident erupts in 1947 and the government cracks down brutally on dissidents, Hinomi's brother joins the resistance and is eventually killed, while Wen-ching is later arrested and disappears, leaving Hinomi searching for him in vain.

wins.title: Rosencrantz & Guildenstern Are Dead
wins.plotSummary: The film, like the play, focuses on Rosencrantz and Guildenstern and their actions (or lack thereof) within the play of Hamlet. The film begins as they travel on horseback to Elsinore, contemplating fate, memory and language. Rosencrantz finds and continually flips a coin which always comes up heads (until he loses the coin, which is replaced by their own coins), causing Guildenstern to conclude that something is wrong with reality.
